Outliers – Complete Notes¶

🔹 What is an Outlier?¶

  • A data point that is very different from other observations
  • Example: The point where a student studied 9 hours but scored 30 marks is an outlier because it doesn’t follow the usual pattern most students who study more hours score higher, but this one scored much lower than expected.

In this dataset, most phones follow a normal trend: as RAM increases, the price also increases. For example:

  • Samsung A1: 2 GB → $100
  • OnePlus 7: 6 GB → $400
  • OnePlus 8: 10 GB → $850

This shows a predictable pattern: higher RAM usually means a higher price.

However, two phones break this trend:

  • iPhone (4 GB RAM, $1000): This phone is much more expensive than expected for its RAM. Normal phones with 4 GB RAM cost around $200–$300.

    • ✅ Outlier on the high-price side.

  • Redmi Note (12 GB RAM, $200): This phone is much cheaper than expected for its RAM. Normal phones with 12 GB RAM cost close to $1000.

    • ✅ Outlier on the low-price side.

Key idea: Outliers are points that deviate significantly from the expected trend. Here, the expected trend is: more RAM → higher price.

🔹 Why Outliers Matter¶

  • Can distort machine learning models
  • Affect accuracy and predictions

🔹 Impact on ML Algorithms¶

Highly Affected Less Affected
1) Linear Regression 1) Decision Trees
2) Logistic Regression 2) Random Forest
3) K-Means Clustering 3) Gradient Boosting
4) Neural Networks / Deep Learning

Why? Affected ones depend on distance/weights.

🔹 Are Outliers Always Bad? ❌ No!¶

Remove When Keep When
1) Clearly a data error (e.g., age = 830) 1) They are meaningful
2) Noise / measurement error 2) Fraud detection
3) Anomaly detection

🔹 Challenges with Outliers¶

  • Easy to detect ❌
  • Hard to decide: Remove? Keep? Modify?

Handeling the Outliers¶

1) Z-Score Method for Outlier Detection¶

📌 Requirement: Data Must Be Normally Distributed¶

  • The Z-score method only works when the data follows a normal (bell-curve) distribution.
  • In a normal distribution:
    • ~68% of data falls within ±1 standard deviation of the mean
    • ~95% falls within ±2 standard deviations
    • ~99.7% falls within ±3 standard deviations

⚠️ If your column is skewed (not symmetric), Z-score is not the right method. Use IQR instead.

📌 The Z-Score Formula¶

$$ Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} $$

Where:

  • $x$ = individual data point
  • $\mu$ = mean of the column
  • $\sigma$ = standard deviation of the column

Interpretation:

  • Z-score tells you how many standard deviations a data point is from the mean.
  • If $Z > 3$ or $Z < -3$ → the data point is an outlier.
  • The safe range is: $-3 \leq Z \leq 3$

📌 Calculating Boundaries (Upper & Lower Limits)¶

Instead of computing Z for every point, you can directly compute the boundary values:

$$ \text{Upper Limit} = \mu + 3\sigma $$ $$ \text{Lower Limit} = \mu - 3\sigma $$

Any value above the upper limit or below the lower limit is an outlier.

📌 Outlier Treatment Techniques¶

Once outliers are identified, there are two main ways to handle them:

1. Trimming (Removal)¶

  • Simply remove the rows containing outlier values.
  • Use when outliers are due to errors or noise.

2. Capping (Winsorization)¶

  • Replace outlier values with the nearest boundary value.
  • Values above upper limit → set to upper limit
  • Values below lower limit → set to lower limit
  • Preserves the number of rows in the dataset.

📌 Key Reminders¶

Point Detail
When to use Z-score Only when the column is normally distributed
Threshold Data outside ±3 standard deviations is an outlier
Trimming Removes rows → dataset shrinks
Capping Replaces values → dataset size stays same
Not suitable for Skewed distributions (use IQR method instead)

📌 Practical Implementation – Z-Score (Trimming & Capping)¶

We'll use the Titanic dataset from seaborn a real-world dataset. We'll apply Z-score outlier detection on the age column (which is approximately normally distributed).

In [3]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Load Titanic dataset
df = sns.load_dataset('titanic')
df = df[['age', 'fare', 'pclass', 'survived' ]].dropna()  # keep relevant cols, drop nulls

print("Shape:", df.shape)
df.head()

Shape: (714, 4)
Out[3]:
age fare pclass survived
0 22.0 7.2500 3 0
1 38.0 71.2833 1 1
2 26.0 7.9250 3 1
3 35.0 53.1000 1 1
4 35.0 8.0500 3 0

Step 1: Check if age, fare, pclass column is normally distributed

In [4]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 6))

axes = axes.flatten()

sns.histplot(df['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Age')

sns.histplot(df['fare'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Distribution of Fare')

sns.histplot(df['pclass'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[2])
axes[2].set_title('Distribution of Pclass')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [5]:
import scipy.stats as stats

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 6))

# Q-Q plot for Age
stats.probplot(df['age'].dropna(), dist="norm", plot=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('Q-Q Plot (Age)')

# Q-Q plot for Fare
stats.probplot(df['fare'].dropna(), dist="norm", plot=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Q-Q Plot (Fare)')

# Q-Q plot for Pclass
stats.probplot(df['pclass'].dropna(), dist="norm", plot=axes[2])
axes[2].set_title('Q-Q Plot (Pclass)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Outlier Analysis Summary¶

Only age appears to follow a normal distribution.
fare and pclass are not normally distributed, so the Z-score method is not suitable for outlier removal.

In [6]:
df[['age', 'fare', 'pclass']].describe()
Out[6]:
age fare pclass
count 714.000000 714.000000 714.000000

std 14.526497 52.918930 0.838250
min 0.420000 0.000000 1.000000
25% 20.125000 8.050000 1.000000
50% 28.000000 15.741700 2.000000
75% 38.000000 33.375000 3.000000
max 80.000000 512.329200 3.000000

Step 2: Calculate Upper & Lower Limits using Z-score (mean ± 3σ)

In [7]:
mean = df['age'].mean()
std = df['age'].std()

upper_limit = int(mean + 3 * std)
lower_limit = int(mean - 3 * std)

print(f"Mean: {mean:.2f}")
print(f"Std:  {std:.2f}")
print(f"Upper Limit: {upper_limit}")
print(f"Lower Limit: {lower_limit}")

Mean: 29.70
Std:  14.53
Upper Limit: 73
Lower Limit: -13

Step 3: Find outliers

In [8]:
# Find outliers
outliers = df[(df['age'] > upper_limit) | (df['age'] < lower_limit)]
print(f"Total rows: {df.shape[0]}")
print(f"Outliers found: {outliers.shape[0]}")
outliers

Total rows: 714
Outliers found: 2
Out[8]:
age fare pclass survived
630 80.0 30.000 1 1
851 74.0 7.775 3 0

Technique 1: Trimming (Remove Outlier Rows)¶

In [9]:
# Trimming: Remove rows with outliers
df_trimmed = df[(df['age'] >= lower_limit) & (df['age'] <= upper_limit)]

print(f"Before trimming: {df.shape[0]} rows")
print(f"After trimming:  {df_trimmed.shape[0]} rows")
print(f"Rows removed:    {df.shape[0] - df_trimmed.shape[0]}")

Before trimming: 714 rows
After trimming:  712 rows
Rows removed:    2
In [10]:
# Compare distribution: Before vs After Trimming
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes = axes.flatten()

sns.histplot(df['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[0], color='salmon')
axes[0].set_title('Before Trimming')
axes[0].axvline(upper_limit, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Upper: {upper_limit:.1f}')
axes[0].axvline(lower_limit, color='blue', linestyle='--', label=f'Lower: {lower_limit:.1f}')

sns.histplot(df_trimmed['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[1], color='skyblue')
axes[1].set_title('After Trimming')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Technique 2: Capping (Replace Outliers with Boundary Values)¶

In [11]:
df_capped = df.copy()

df_capped['age'] = df_capped['age'].apply(
    lambda x: upper_limit if x > upper_limit else (lower_limit if x < lower_limit else x)
)

print(f"Before capping - Age range: {df['age'].min():.1f} to {df['age'].max():.1f}")
print(f"After capping  - Age range: {df_capped['age'].min():.1f} to {df_capped['age'].max():.1f}")

Before capping - Age range: 0.4 to 80.0
After capping  - Age range: 0.4 to 73.0
In [12]:
# Compare distribution: Before vs After Capping
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

sns.histplot(df['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[0], color='salmon')
axes[0].axvline(upper_limit, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Upper: {upper_limit:.1f}')
axes[0].axvline(lower_limit, color='blue', linestyle='--', label=f'Lower: {lower_limit:.1f}')
axes[0].set_title('Before Capping')
axes[0].legend()

sns.histplot(df_capped['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[1], color='lightgreen')
axes[1].set_title('After Capping')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Side-by-Side Comparison: Trimming vs Capping¶

In [13]:
# Final comparison: Original vs Trimmed vs Capped distributions
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 4))

axes = axes.flatten()

# Original
sns.histplot(df['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[0], color='salmon')
axes[0].set_title(f'Original ({df.shape[0]} rows)')

# After trimming
sns.histplot(df_trimmed['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[1], color='skyblue')
axes[1].set_title(f'After Trimming ({df_trimmed.shape[0]} rows)')

# After capping
sns.histplot(df_capped['age'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[2], color='lightgreen')
axes[2].set_title(f'After Capping ({df_capped.shape[0]} rows)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Summary stats comparison
summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Original': df['age'].describe(),
    'Trimmed': df_trimmed['age'].describe(),
    'Capped': df_capped['age'].describe()
}).round(2)

summary
Out[13]:
Original Trimmed Capped
count 714.00 712.00 714.00
mean 29.70 29.57 29.69
std 14.53 14.33 14.49
min 0.42 0.42 0.42
25% 20.12 20.00 20.12
50% 28.00 28.00 28.00
75% 38.00 38.00 38.00
max 80.00 71.00 73.00

2) IQR (BOX PLOT) Method for Outlier Detection¶

📌 Why IQR?¶

  • Z-score works only for normally distributed data.
  • For skewed data, use the IQR (Interquartile Range) method.

📌 What is IQR?¶

  • Q1 (25th percentile): 25% of data falls below this value
  • Q3 (75th percentile): 75% of data falls below this value
  • IQR = Q3 - Q1 → the range of the middle 50% of data

📌 Calculating Boundaries¶

$$ \text{Lower Limit} = Q1 - 1.5 \times IQR $$ $$ \text{Upper Limit} = Q3 + 1.5 \times IQR $$

Any value below the lower limit or above the upper limit is an outlier.

The 1.5 multiplier is the standard convention (used in boxplots). Use 3×IQR for detecting only extreme outliers.

📌 Key Reminders¶

Point Detail
When to use IQR Works for any distribution (normal or skewed)
Based on Percentiles (Q1, Q3), not mean/std
Threshold 1.5×IQR (standard) or 3×IQR (extreme only)
Trimming Remove rows outside limits → dataset shrinks
Capping Replace outlier values with boundary limits → size preserved

📌 Practical Implementation – IQR (Trimming & Capping)¶

We'll use the fare column from the same Titanic dataset — it was skewed (not normal), so Z-score won't work here. IQR is the right choice.

Step 1: Confirm fare is skewed (not suitable for Z-score)

In [15]:
# Create figure
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

# Histogram
sns.histplot(df['fare'], kde=True, bins=30, color='orange', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Fare (Skewed)')

# Boxplot
sns.boxplot(x=df['fare'], color='orange', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Boxplot of Fare')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Skewness
print(f"Skewness: {df['fare'].skew():.2f}")  # high skewness → IQR is better

Skewness: 4.65

Step 2: Calculate Q1, Q3, IQR and Boundaries

In [16]:
Q1 = df['fare'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df['fare'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1

lower_limit_iqr = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
upper_limit_iqr = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

print(f"Q1 (25th percentile): {Q1:.2f}")
print(f"Q3 (75th percentile): {Q3:.2f}")
print(f"IQR (Q3 - Q1):        {IQR:.2f}")
print(f"Lower Limit:           {lower_limit_iqr:.2f}")
print(f"Upper Limit:           {upper_limit_iqr:.2f}")

Q1 (25th percentile): 8.05
Q3 (75th percentile): 33.38
IQR (Q3 - Q1):        25.32
Lower Limit:           -29.94
Upper Limit:           71.36

Step 3: Find outliers

In [18]:
# Find outliers using IQR
outliers_iqr = df[(df['fare'] > upper_limit_iqr) | (df['fare'] < lower_limit_iqr)]
print(f"Total rows: {df.shape[0]}")
print(f"Outliers found: {outliers_iqr.shape[0]}")
outliers_iqr['fare'].head(10)

Total rows: 714
Outliers found: 94
Out[18]:
27     263.0000
34      82.1708
52      76.7292
61      80.0000
62      83.4750
72      73.5000
88     263.0000
102     77.2875
118    247.5208
120     73.5000
Name: fare, dtype: float64

Technique 1: Trimming (Remove Outlier Rows)¶

In [19]:
# Trimming: Remove rows with outlier fare values
df_trimmed_iqr = df[(df['fare'] >= lower_limit_iqr) & (df['fare'] <= upper_limit_iqr)]

print(f"Before trimming: {df.shape[0]} rows")
print(f"After trimming:  {df_trimmed_iqr.shape[0]} rows")
print(f"Rows removed:    {df.shape[0] - df_trimmed_iqr.shape[0]}")

Before trimming: 714 rows
After trimming:  620 rows
Rows removed:    94
In [20]:
# Compare distribution: Before vs After Trimming
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

sns.histplot(df['fare'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[0], color='salmon')
axes[0].axvline(upper_limit_iqr, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Upper: {upper_limit_iqr:.1f}')
axes[0].axvline(lower_limit_iqr, color='blue', linestyle='--', label=f'Lower: {lower_limit_iqr:.1f}')
axes[0].set_title('Before Trimming (Fare)')
axes[0].legend()

sns.histplot(df_trimmed_iqr['fare'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[1], color='skyblue')
axes[1].set_title('After Trimming (Fare)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Technique 2: Capping (Replace Outliers with Boundary Values)¶

In [21]:
# Capping: Replace outlier fare values with boundary limits
df_capped_iqr = df.copy()

df_capped_iqr['fare'] = df_capped_iqr['fare'].apply(
    lambda x: upper_limit_iqr if x > upper_limit_iqr else (lower_limit_iqr if x < lower_limit_iqr else x)
)

print(f"Before capping - Fare range: {df['fare'].min():.2f} to {df['fare'].max():.2f}")
print(f"After capping  - Fare range: {df_capped_iqr['fare'].min():.2f} to {df_capped_iqr['fare'].max():.2f}")
print(f"Rows preserved: {df_capped_iqr.shape[0]} (no rows removed!)")

Before capping - Fare range: 0.00 to 512.33
After capping  - Fare range: 0.00 to 71.36
Rows preserved: 714 (no rows removed!)
In [22]:
# Compare distribution: Before vs After Capping
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

sns.histplot(df['fare'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[0], color='salmon')
axes[0].axvline(upper_limit_iqr, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Upper: {upper_limit_iqr:.1f}')
axes[0].axvline(lower_limit_iqr, color='blue', linestyle='--', label=f'Lower: {lower_limit_iqr:.1f}')
axes[0].set_title('Before Capping (Fare)')
axes[0].legend()

sns.histplot(df_capped_iqr['fare'], kde=True, bins=30, ax=axes[1], color='lightgreen')
axes[1].set_title('After Capping (Fare)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Side-by-Side Comparison: Original vs Trimmed vs Capped (IQR)¶

In [23]:
# Final comparison: Original vs Trimmed vs Capped (Boxplots)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 4))

sns.boxplot(x=df['fare'], ax=axes[0], color='salmon')
axes[0].set_title(f'Original ({df.shape[0]} rows)')

sns.boxplot(x=df_trimmed_iqr['fare'], ax=axes[1], color='skyblue')
axes[1].set_title(f'After Trimming ({df_trimmed_iqr.shape[0]} rows)')

sns.boxplot(x=df_capped_iqr['fare'], ax=axes[2], color='lightgreen')
axes[2].set_title(f'After Capping ({df_capped_iqr.shape[0]} rows)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Summary stats comparison
summary_iqr = pd.DataFrame({
    'Original': df['fare'].describe(),
    'Trimmed (IQR)': df_trimmed_iqr['fare'].describe(),
    'Capped (IQR)': df_capped_iqr['fare'].describe()
}).round(2)

summary_iqr
